one_line_takeaway: A 3,250-scenario controlled comparison showing Function Calling and MCP carry opposite vulnerability profiles (system-centric vs LLM-centric), that chained multi-stage attacks reach 91-96% success where single-vector attacks manage only ~50%, and that smarter reasoning models detect attacks better yet are exploited more once breached.
key_finding: Architecture fundamentally reshapes the threat landscape rather than uniformly raising or lowering risk. Function Calling had higher overall ASR (73.5%) than MCP (62.59%) and far higher system-centric ASR (87.98% vs MCP 57%), but the picture inverts for LLM-centric attacks where MCP was most exposed (68.28% vs Function Calling 59%). Attack complexity dominates: simple attacks succeeded only ~50% of the time (DoS at 0% due to cloud interception), yet chained 5-step attacks reached 91-96% ASR across all configs. Composed attacks diverged by architecture -- Function Calling amplified (56%->70%) while MCP contained them (60%->51.32%) -- and composition Ap(ADoS(x)) revived DoS attacks that clouds otherwise blocked. Counterintuitively, reasoning-capable models (O1, O3-mini, Claude 3-7) showed the BEST initial threat detection (17.8% avg RR) but the HIGHEST overall exploitability once breached (Claude 3-5 peaked at 75% ASR, GPT 4.1 at 72%), and a 9.44% gap between Claude 3-5 (75%) and Claude Haiku (65.56%) under identical ASL-2 safety policy shows static safety tiers do not predict deployed vulnerability.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is the first controlled, head-to-head empirical security comparison of two LLM-agent tool-calling deployment paradigms: Function Calling (centralized orchestration) versus the Model Context Protocol (MCP, distributed client-server). To make the comparison rigorous, the authors build three of their own artifacts: (1) a unified threat classification framework that maps nine agent threat categories (T1-T9, e.g. Instruction Hijacking, Tool Schema Tampering, Runtime Action Abuse, Computational Resource Manipulation, Human Trust Manipulation, Governance Evasion) simultaneously onto the software-security STRIDE taxonomy and the AI-specific ATFAA taxonomy, plus their own seven attack surfaces and six attack vectors; (2) a formal three-tier attack progression model (simple, composed, chained) with explicit probability composition formulas (e.g. chained ASR = product of per-stage conditional success probabilities) and an amplification factor AF = ASR(complex)/ASR(simple); and (3) an extensible, reproducible testing harness. Note: STRIDE, ATFAA, and the compared benchmarks (AgentDojo, InjecAgent, ASB) are prior work the authors build on or measure against, NOT their contribution -- their contribution is the cross-domain synthesis, the paradigm comparison, and the multi-stage attack modeling that those siloed prior works lack.
threat_model: Adversary can inject adversarial content at any of seven attack surfaces of an LLM agent's execution flow, formalized as As = {System prompt S, User prompt U, Tool set T, Function name Fn, Function parameters Fp, Tool output O, Response R}, where R = rho(r(S,U,T), O) and O = Fn(Fp). Attack vectors span both AI-specific threats (direct prompt injection Ap on S/U; indirect prompt injection Aipi embedded in tool output O) and traditional software threats (JSON injection AJSON on Fn/Fp/O; denial-of-service ADoS; man-in-the-middle AMitM on tool comms; tool injection ATi adding unauthorized tools). Attacks escalate from simple (single surface, single technique) to composed (nested techniques, e.g. Ap weakens validation so AJSON succeeds) to chained (5-stage path traversing Input->Configuration->Execution->Output->Response nodes). Systems are cloud-hosted (Azure/AWS) finance and healthcare agents run in DEFAULT, unhardened configurations so that measured vulnerability reflects intrinsic architectural properties, not custom hardening.
limitations: Authors' stated limits: seven models across only two providers (Azure/AWS) is substantial but not exhaustive and excludes emerging models; scope covers only Function Calling and MCP, missing decentralized multi-agent protocols (A2A, ACP, MARCO, Microsoft orchestration) with peer-to-peer trust surfaces; the nine-vector threat framework may miss novel attack categories; findings are a temporal snapshot that model/defense updates can invalidate; and only cloud-API deployments are covered, not edge/hybrid/custom. Reviewer-added caveats: many quantitative claims (per-vector rates, chain-depth curves) are reported only via figures rather than full tables, limiting independent verification; the ASR/RR ground truth depends heavily on an LLM-as-judge (DeepSeek-R1, also one of the attack generators) despite good human agreement; 'default/unhardened configuration' inflates absolute ASR versus production systems with guardrails; and the composition/chaining probability formulas are presented as theory but the paper does not clearly validate the multiplicative model against measured stage-wise data.

datasets_used: 3,250 self-generated attack test scenarios (no external benchmark dataset; cases authored by the study)
Finance and healthcare agent domains (critical-infrastructure contexts)
300-case (10%) human-expert-labeled validation subset for judge calibration
Attack complexity split: 40% simple, 30% composed, 30% chained
quantitative_results: Overall ASR: Function Calling 73.5% vs MCP 62.59%
System-centric ASR: Function Calling 87.98% vs MCP 57%
LLM-centric ASR: MCP 68.28% (highest) vs Function Calling 59%
Azure Function Calling: LLM-centric 55.42%, system-centric 92.27% (~74% overall)
AWS Function Calling: LLM-centric 63%, system-centric 84.76% (~74% overall)
Chained attacks: 91-96% ASR across all configs (5-step chains)
Chain-depth ASR: Azure FC 35% (1-step) -> 95% (5-step); AWS MCP 51% -> 91%
Simple attacks ~50% ASR; DoS 0% ASR when simple but bypassed via composed Ap(ADoS(x))
Reasoning models (O1, O3-mini, Claude 3-7) avg RR 17.8% (best initial detection)
Per-model ASR: Claude 3-5 75%, GPT 4.1 72%, Claude Haiku 65.56% (RR 18.37%)
9.44% ASR gap between Claude 3-5 and Claude Haiku under identical ASL-2 policy
RR degradation with chain depth: Azure FC 50% -> 32%; AWS MCP 40% -> 33%
Reported defense mitigations cited: IsolateGPT <30% overhead; guardrails reduce LLM-centric success by 40%
baselines_compared: AgentDojo (92% DPI/isolated-attack defense effectiveness, but inadequate vs composed attacks)
Agent Security Bench / ASB (56.44% average attack success rate)
This paper's own simple-attack rates positioned between them: FC 45.3%, MCP 38.7%
All three baselines have 0% cross-domain isolation coverage (the identified gap)
